questions.prompt: How did globular cluster observations help Harlow Shapley reason about the Sun's location?
choices: Their uneven apparent distribution suggested the Sun is not at the centre of the Milky Way.
Their even spacing around Earth showed Earth is near the galaxy's centre.
Their position along the ecliptic showed the Milky Way is a solar system.
Their motion through the zodiac showed the Sun is outside the galaxy.
questions.explanation: Shapley used the distribution of globular clusters to infer that we are away from the galaxy's centre.

questions.prompt: Why were radio observations useful for mapping the Milky Way?
choices: Radio waves can travel through gas and dust that block some visible light.
Radio waves show the Milky Way in ordinary visible colours.
Radio maps work by ignoring gas and dust instead of detecting signals.
Radio telescopes are useful mainly for finding planets near the Sun.
questions.explanation: Radio mapping helped reveal the disk-like structure of the Milky Way.

kp-511.title: KP7: Local Group and larger structures

questions.prompt: What is the Local Group?
choices: A star cluster inside one spiral arm of the Milky Way.
The set of zodiac constellations visible from Canada.
A group of galaxies that includes the Milky Way and Andromeda.
A group of planets orbiting the Sun.
questions.explanation: The Milky Way is part of a larger group of galaxies called the Local Group.

questions.prompt: Which sequence correctly places the Milky Way in a larger structure?
choices: Milky Way -> Solar System -> Local Group.
Milky Way -> zodiac constellations -> Virgo Supercluster.
Milky Way -> star cluster -> single star.
Milky Way -> Local Group -> Virgo Supercluster.
questions.explanation: The Local Group belongs to the still larger Virgo Supercluster.

questions.prompt: Why is Andromeda useful when discussing the Local Group?
choices: It is a major neighbouring galaxy in the same group as the Milky Way.
It is a star cluster that helped Shapley map the Milky Way.
It is the bright band of stars seen within the Milky Way.
It is a zodiac constellation used to locate planets.
questions.explanation: Andromeda and the Milky Way are two large galaxies in the Local Group.

questions.prompt: What does \(1\,AU\) represent?
choices: The distance light travels in one year.
The average distance between Earth and the Sun, about \(150\,000\,000\,km\).
The diameter of the Milky Way.
The distance from the Sun to the nearest star.
questions.explanation: One astronomical unit is based on the Earth-Sun distance.

questions.prompt: A planet is about \(4\,AU\) from the Sun. About how far is that in kilometres if \(1\,AU \approx 150\,000\,000\,km\)?
When is the astronomical unit most useful?
choices: When measuring the distance between distant galaxies.
When naming types of galaxies.
When classifying visible star colours.
When describing distances within the Solar System.
questions.explanation: AU is convenient for Solar System distances, while light-years are better for stars and galaxies.

questions.prompt: What is a light-year?
choices: The time Earth takes to orbit the Sun once.
The distance light travels in one year.
The age of light after it leaves a nearby star.
The average distance between Earth and the Sun.
questions.explanation: A light-year is a distance unit, not a time unit.

questions.prompt: Why are light-years more useful than kilometres for distances between stars?
choices: Kilometres are useful but would create extremely large numbers for interstellar distances.
Light-years are used because they make the stars physically closer together.
Kilometres apply within the Solar System, while light-years are used for star and galaxy distances.
Light-years are used mainly because light travels slower between stars.
questions.explanation: A light-year represents a very large distance, about \(9.46 \times 10^{12}\,km\).

questions.prompt: A star is listed as 12 light-years away. What does that distance mean?
choices: The star is 12 years old.
The star is 12 astronomical units from the Sun.
Light from the star takes about 12 years to reach Earth.
Earth takes 12 years to orbit that star.
questions.explanation: Distance in light-years also tells us how long the light has travelled.

questions.prompt: About how fast does light travel through space?
questions.explanation: The speed of light in space is about \(300\,000\,km/s\).

questions.prompt: If light travels for 3 seconds, about how far does it go in space?
questions.explanation: Use distance = speed x time: \(300\,000\,km/s \times 3\,s = 900\,000\,km\).

questions.prompt: What does the speed of light help explain about astronomy?
choices: Light is fast, but space distances are large enough that travel time still matters.
Light speed makes nearby and distant objects equally current to observers.
Stars are close enough that light travel time is usually ignored beyond the Solar System.
Visible light travels through space, while other electromagnetic waves do not.
questions.explanation: Space is so large that travel time matters even for light.

questions.prompt: Why do astronomers say that looking at distant objects is looking back in time?
choices: A telescope changes old light into present-day light.
Distant objects age more slowly because light travels through space.
The light left the object in the past and took time to reach us.
Light-years measure how old an object is rather than how far away it is.
questions.explanation: We see objects as they were when the light began travelling toward Earth.

questions.prompt: If a star is 40 light-years away, how old is the light we see from it?
choices: About 40 seconds old.
About 4 years old.
About 40 years old.
About 400 million years old.
questions.explanation: A distance of 40 light-years means the light travelled for about 40 years.

questions.prompt: Which statement best corrects 'We see the Sun exactly as it is right now'?
choices: The Sun is close enough that light travel time matters mainly beyond the Solar System.
The Sun's light reaches Earth quickly enough that telescope processing is the main delay.
The Sun is seen in real time because it is inside the Milky Way.
Sunlight takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, so we see the Sun slightly in the past.
questions.explanation: Even the Sun is not seen in real time because light has a finite speed.

questions.prompt: What is parallax?
choices: The change in a star's brightness after its light travels for many years.
The distance light travels in one year through empty space.
The apparent shift of an object's position when viewed from two different lines of sight.
The shift in a planet's actual orbit caused by Earth's gravity.
questions.explanation: Parallax is an apparent position shift caused by a change in viewpoint.

questions.prompt: In a classroom parallax model, your thumb seems to jump against a distant wall when you switch eyes. What is changing?
choices: The actual location of the wall.
Your line of sight to the thumb.
The temperature of the background object.
The distance light travels in one year.
questions.explanation: Switching eyes changes the viewing position, which creates the apparent shift.

questions.prompt: How does parallax generally change when the object is moved closer to the observer?
choices: The apparent shift becomes smaller because nearby objects are easier to focus on.
The apparent shift stays the same if the background is distant.
The apparent shift becomes larger.
The shift becomes a measure of luminosity instead of distance.
questions.explanation: Nearby objects show a larger parallax shift than very distant objects.

questions.prompt: How do astronomers use Earth's orbit in stellar parallax?
choices: They use Earth's atmosphere as a lens to move the star's apparent position.
They compare the star's position with the Moon's phase over one month.
They use Earth's orbit to make the star physically closer after six months.
They observe a star from opposite sides of Earth's orbit to create a wide baseline.
questions.explanation: Earth's orbit provides two widely separated viewpoints for triangulation.

questions.prompt: Why does parallax become less useful for very distant stars?
choices: Distant stars are too luminous for geometry to apply.
The apparent shift becomes extremely small and difficult to measure accurately.
The baseline of Earth's orbit becomes too large for triangulation.
The parallax shift becomes confused with the star's colour temperature.
questions.explanation: As distance increases, parallax angle decreases.

questions.prompt: Why do satellites such as HIPPARCOS and Gaia improve distance measurements?
choices: They make stars closer by observing them from orbit instead of the ground.
They measure distance by recording sound waves from stars.
They replace triangulation with brightness estimates for large groups of stars.
They can measure tiny star positions and parallax shifts with high precision.
questions.explanation: Precise instruments improve measurements of very small angular shifts.

questions.prompt: Which list contains important properties used to compare stars?
choices: Constellation name, zodiac sign, telescope type, and season.
Colour, temperature, luminosity, mass, and brightness.
Distance from Earth alone, because other properties depend on distance.
Apparent brightness, planet count, parallax angle, and galaxy shape.
questions.explanation: Astronomers compare multiple properties to understand stars and their life cycles.

questions.prompt: Why are star properties useful to astronomers?
choices: They are useful mainly for naming stars in constellations.
They matter for nearby stars but not for distant stars in the Milky Way.
They describe what stars look like but not how they change over time.
They help astronomers compare stars and infer stages of stellar development.
questions.explanation: Patterns in star properties help explain how stars form, change, and end.

questions.prompt: Which observation alone is least enough to fully compare two stars?
choices: Their colour.
Their temperature.
How bright they look from Earth.
Their mass.
questions.explanation: Apparent brightness depends on distance as well as actual energy output.

questions.prompt: What does a star's colour tell astronomers?
choices: It gives the star's exact distance in astronomical units.
It directly gives the star's total mass without other data.
It identifies the star's galaxy type by shape.
It gives evidence about the star's surface temperature.
questions.explanation: Star colour is related to surface temperature.

questions.prompt: Which star colour usually indicates the hottest surface temperature?
choices: Red.
Orange.
Blue or bluish-white.
Yellowish like the Sun.
questions.explanation: Bluish stars are hotter than reddish stars.

questions.prompt: A student says, 'Red stars are hotter because red looks like fire.' What is the best correction?
choices: Colour is related to temperature mainly after a star's distance is known.
Red and blue stars can be compared by brightness but not temperature.
In stars, red generally indicates a cooler surface than blue.
Blue stars appear hotter because distance makes their colour look cooler.
questions.explanation: The everyday association of red with heat can be misleading for stellar colour.

questions.prompt: What is luminosity?
choices: How bright a star looks from Earth to an observer.
The total amount of energy a star gives off each second.
The star's distance from Earth measured in light-years.
The apparent size of the star in a telescope image.
questions.explanation: Luminosity describes a star's actual energy output.

questions.prompt: Two identical stars have the same luminosity, but Star A looks brighter from Earth than Star B. What is a likely reason?
choices: Star A must have a lower surface temperature.
Star B must be a red star while Star A must be blue.
Star B is less luminous even though the stars were described as identical.
Star A is closer to Earth.
questions.explanation: Apparent brightness depends on distance as well as luminosity.

questions.prompt: Which statement avoids a common misconception?
choices: Apparent brightness and luminosity are the same if two stars have the same colour.
A nearby modest star can look brighter than a distant highly luminous star.
Distance affects parallax but not how bright a star appears.
The brightest-looking star in the sky must have the greatest energy output.
questions.explanation: A nearby modest star can look brighter than a distant highly luminous star.

questions.prompt: What does one solar mass mean?
choices: The mass of Earth.
A mass equal to the mass of the Sun.
The distance between Earth and the Sun.
The luminosity of the Sun compared with other stars.
questions.explanation: Stellar masses are often compared with the Sun's mass.

questions.prompt: Why is stellar mass important in predicting a star's future?
choices: Mass matters mainly because heavier stars tend to look closer from Earth.
Mass is useful for planets but not for star life cycles.
Mass determines apparent brightness without needing luminosity or distance.
Mass strongly affects how long a star remains active and what final stage it may reach.
questions.explanation: Initial mass is one of the most important factors in stellar evolution.

questions.prompt: Which observation helped astronomers determine masses of many stars?
choices: Measuring a single star's colour from one photograph.
Studying binary stars that orbit each other.
Using a planisphere to trace zodiac signs.
Comparing galaxy shape categories.
questions.explanation: The orbits of binary stars provide information that can be used to calculate stellar mass.

questions.prompt: What does an H-R diagram compare?
choices: Planet distances, moon phases, and zodiac dates.
Telescope wavelengths, prices, and mirror sizes.
Star properties such as colour, temperature, luminosity, and absolute magnitude.
Galaxy shapes and the number of planets in each galaxy.
questions.explanation: An H-R diagram is a graph used to show relationships among star properties.

questions.prompt: Why was the H-R diagram useful to astronomers?
choices: It proved that colour and temperature are unrelated.
It works by plotting a star's distance from Earth against its constellation name.
It revealed patterns in star data that helped classify stars and study stellar evolution.
It is useful mainly because it lists stars in alphabetical order.
questions.explanation: The H-R diagram organizes star data so important patterns become visible.

questions.prompt: On a simplified H-R diagram, a very hot and very luminous star is usually found toward which region?
choices: The lower-right region.
The upper-left region.
The lower-left region with faint hot stars.
The upper-right region with cool luminous stars.
questions.explanation: H-R diagrams commonly place hotter stars toward the left and more luminous stars higher up.

questions.prompt: What is the main sequence on an H-R diagram?
choices: A region where cool but very luminous stars are grouped above the central band.
A diagonal band where many visible stars are arranged by temperature and luminosity.
A scale that ranks stars by apparent brightness as seen from Earth.
A pathway showing the order in which planets formed around a star.
questions.explanation: The main sequence is the central diagonal band on an H-R diagram and includes about 90% of stars visible from Earth.

questions.prompt: About what fraction of stars visible from Earth are on the main sequence?
choices: About \(50\%\), because the diagram is split into hot and cool halves.
About \(25\%\), because the hottest stars dominate the upper-left of the diagram.
questions.explanation: The textbook states that the main sequence accounts for about 90% of stars visible from Earth.

questions.prompt: Why are red giants and supergiants not simply ordinary main sequence stars?
choices: They are plotted above the main sequence because they are cool but very luminous.
They sit on the lower right of the main sequence because they are dim red stars.
They are placed on the H-R diagram by apparent brightness rather than luminosity.
They belong to the main sequence if their surface temperature is red.
questions.explanation: Their position on the H-R diagram helps astronomers infer that stars can change over time.

kp-524.title: KP7: Star life cycles and initial mass

questions.prompt: What mainly determines a star's life-cycle pathway?
choices: Its surface colour after it has already become a red giant.
Its initial mass on the main sequence.
Its apparent magnitude as seen from Earth on one night.
Its distance from the nearest constellation boundary.
questions.explanation: Initial mass strongly controls how fast a star uses fuel and what it becomes.

questions.prompt: Compared with lower-mass stars, high-mass stars generally use their fuel how?
choices: More quickly, because their cores reach conditions for rapid fusion.
More slowly, because greater mass spreads the fuel through a larger volume.
At a similar rate, but their greater brightness mostly reflects distance.
Mostly after the red supergiant stage, when fusion first begins.
questions.explanation: High-mass stars are very luminous and consume fuel rapidly.

questions.prompt: Which comparison best fits star life-cycle pathways?
choices: Low-mass stars usually become supernovas, while high-mass stars fade directly into white dwarfs.
Intermediate-mass stars avoid red giant stages because their fuel lasts too long.
Low- and intermediate-mass stars tend toward white dwarf endings, while very massive stars can lead to neutron stars or black holes.
High-mass and low-mass stars follow the same pathway but at different distances from Earth.
questions.explanation: Different mass ranges follow different evolutionary routes.

questions.prompt: Which list contains possible final stages or remnants of stars?
choices: Nebula, main sequence star, and red giant.
White dwarf, neutron star, and black hole.
Spectral type, absolute magnitude, and luminosity.
Binary star, solar mass, and constellation.
questions.explanation: A star's mass and evolution can lead to these final remnants.

questions.prompt: What can happen to a star like the Sun after it becomes a red giant?
choices: Its red outer layers can collapse directly into a neutron star.
Its luminosity can rise enough to produce a high-mass supernova.
Its core can shrink while outer gas drifts away, leaving a white dwarf.
Its surface can cool until nuclear fusion begins for the first time.
questions.explanation: Intermediate-mass stars can expand into red giants and later leave white dwarf cores.

questions.prompt: A very massive star explodes as a supernova. What kind of remnant could remain?
choices: A white dwarf that slowly cools because the core was not compressed strongly.
A new main sequence star with the same initial mass as before.
A red giant that has not yet used up hydrogen in its core.
A neutron star or black hole, depending on the remaining core mass.
questions.explanation: The collapsed core after a massive-star supernova may become a neutron star or black hole.

questions.prompt: Why are supernovas important for the chemical history of the universe?
choices: They convert heavy elements back into hydrogen before new stars form.
They keep heavy elements trapped permanently inside a collapsed core.
They make heavy elements by cooling ordinary space between galaxies.
They can eject heavy elements into space where later stars and planets can form.
questions.explanation: Supernovas spread elements made in massive stars into surrounding space.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains how elements in planets and living things can be connected to older stars?
choices: Elements in planets are unrelated to stars because planets form after stars finish evolving.
Some heavier elements were formed in stars and scattered by supernovas before becoming part of new systems.
Heavy elements form mainly when light from nearby stars reaches a young planet.
A planet's gravity changes hydrogen into carbon and oxygen after the planet cools.
questions.explanation: Material from earlier generations of stars can be recycled into later stars, planets, and life.

questions.prompt: What happens in a supernova?
choices: The outer portion of a massive star is blasted outward in an enormous explosion.
A low-mass star slowly cools into a black dwarf that has been observed directly.
A nebula condenses into a main sequence star without a collapse event.
A red giant loses surface gas gently without a violent shock wave.
questions.explanation: A supernova is a powerful explosion in the late life of a massive star.

questions.prompt: What was MOST designed to study?
choices: The spectra of galaxies to measure universal expansion.
Small changes in the brightness of Sun-like stars.
The surface rocks of nearby planets using a landing probe.
The positions of constellations for navigation from Earth.
questions.explanation: MOST studied tiny brightness changes to learn about Sun-like stars and their life cycles.

questions.prompt: Why is MOST significant in Canadian space science?
choices: It was Canada's first space telescope.
It was the first Canadian satellite to carry astronauts to orbit.
It was Canada's first telescope to use a mirror larger than Hubble's.
It was a mission designed to photograph most bright stars in the Milky Way.
questions.explanation: MOST stands for Microvariability and Oscillation of Stars and was Canada's first space telescope.

questions.prompt: A student says a small telescope cannot make useful discoveries. How does MOST challenge that idea?
choices: It succeeded because a focused instrument can make precise measurements over long observing times.
It showed that small telescopes are useful mainly when they avoid collecting numerical data.
It worked by replacing careful observation with computer predictions about stars.
It was effective because microsatellites have the same instruments and costs as large observatories.
questions.explanation: MOST shows that small, focused space technology can still provide valuable scientific data.

questions.prompt: Why are tiny changes in star brightness scientifically useful?
choices: They can reveal clues about a star's structure, activity, and life cycle.
They replace the need to compare a star with any long-term data.
They mainly show that a star has moved closer to Earth during the observation.
They are most useful when the star's colour changes from blue to red.
questions.explanation: Careful brightness monitoring can reveal information that short or rough observations miss.

questions.prompt: Why is monitoring the same star for many days or weeks valuable?
choices: It allows astronomers to change the star's brightness in a controlled test.
It works because repeated observations make measurement uncertainty disappear.
It is mainly useful for converting apparent magnitude into spectral type.
It helps detect patterns and small changes over time.
questions.explanation: Long-term data helps astronomers identify subtle variation.

questions.prompt: Which choice best describes a precision measurement in this context?
choices: Detecting a very small change in a star's brightness.
Estimating brightness from a single unaided-eye observation.
Grouping stars by constellation before comparing their light output.
Recording whether a star is visible above the horizon.
questions.explanation: MOST's value came from measuring extremely small brightness changes.

questions.prompt: What is a major advantage of microsatellites compared with very large space telescopes?
choices: They can be smaller and less expensive to build and launch.
They usually avoid the need for a focused mission design.
They collect broader data because compact instruments cover many wavelengths.
They become more useful by staying on Earth's surface.
questions.explanation: Microsatellite design can lower cost and make specialized missions practical.

questions.prompt: Which comparison is most accurate?
choices: MOST had a broader mission than Hubble because it was a microsatellite.
MOST was much smaller and cheaper than Hubble, but it had a focused scientific purpose.
MOST and Hubble measured the same targets in the same way for the same length of time.
MOST was less scientifically useful because useful observations require a school-bus-sized telescope.
questions.explanation: A smaller satellite can still be powerful when its mission is specific.

questions.prompt: What is a possible tradeoff in microsatellite missions?
choices: They may be designed for narrower tasks than large multi-purpose observatories.
They are usually selected when scientists do not need repeated measurements.
They improve precision mainly by having a larger mirror than major observatories.
They avoid tradeoffs because lower mass automatically improves several capabilities.
questions.explanation: Small missions often gain efficiency by focusing on particular measurements.

questions.prompt: Which groups contributed to the MOST project?
choices: Canadian researchers, engineers, agencies, universities, and companies.
Astronomers working separately from engineers after launch was complete.
A single observatory team that collected data without satellite design work.
International telescope operators who used MOST but did not build or manage it.
questions.explanation: MOST was a collaborative Canadian space technology project.

questions.prompt: What broader lesson can MOST support?
choices: A mission's scientific value depends mainly on being larger than previous telescopes.
Canadian space technology is most useful when it repeats the goals of larger missions.
Smaller space technologies can still make important scientific contributions.
A satellite's cost is a better measure of success than the quality of its data.
questions.explanation: MOST is an example of focused, smaller-scale technology contributing to astronomy.

questions.prompt: Why is collaboration important in a mission like MOST?
choices: Collaboration helps connect science goals, engineering design, launch planning, and data use.
Collaboration matters mainly after the mission ends, when scientific data are no longer needed.
Collaboration is useful because it replaces testing with agreement among researchers.
Collaboration makes a mission less specialized by removing engineering constraints.
questions.explanation: Space technology usually requires many kinds of expertise working together.

questions.prompt: Which career connection is directly related to astronomy and space science?
choices: Aerospace engineer designing systems that support satellites or space missions.
Laboratory technician testing soil samples for local garden nutrients.
Civil engineer planning a road without using space or atmospheric data.
Medical technologist calibrating equipment for blood analysis.
questions.explanation: Astronomy connects to careers in science, engineering, education, data, and technology.

questions.prompt: Why might a space educator need strong astronomy knowledge?
choices: They explain space science clearly to students or the public.
They mainly operate radio telescopes without interpreting observations.
They focus on public speaking while scientific accuracy is handled by the audience.
They need astronomy vocabulary mainly for naming constellations in stories.
questions.explanation: Communication careers still require accurate understanding of the science being taught.

questions.prompt: Which statement best avoids a misconception about astronomy careers?
choices: Astronomy careers are limited to nighttime observation through optical telescopes.
Astronomy work includes engineering, communication, robotics, data analysis, and atmospheric science, not just telescope viewing.
Astronomy-related jobs mostly avoid mathematics once observations have been collected.
Astronomy knowledge is useful in museums and planetariums but not in technology careers.
questions.explanation: The field connects with many kinds of work beyond observing through a telescope.

questions.prompt: What is one reason atmospheric scientists monitor space weather?
choices: Space weather mainly changes local air pressure in the same way as daily weather systems.
Solar radiation storms can affect satellites, power grids, and radio communication.
Solar storms are monitored to predict star life cycles in nearby galaxies.
Space weather alerts are used mostly to classify planets by surface temperature.
questions.explanation: Space weather can disturb technology systems near Earth.

questions.prompt: Why can space weather alerts be useful?
choices: They give operators time to prepare for possible effects on technology and communication systems.
They allow scientists to stop solar radiation before it reaches Earth.
They replace satellite monitoring because alerts already contain complete repair instructions.
They predict ordinary rain and wind conditions more accurately than weather stations.
questions.explanation: Alerts help people manage risks to satellites, radio links, and power systems.

questions.prompt: Which example is most connected to space weather?
choices: A solar radiation storm interferes with radio communication.
A local temperature drop changes how quickly water freezes.
A planet's axis tilt changes the length of daylight through a year.
A cloud layer blocks visible light from a ground telescope.
questions.explanation: Solar activity can affect Earth's upper atmosphere and human technology.

questions.prompt: Which set best represents technologies used in space exploration and astronomy?
choices: Ground telescopes, classroom models, and unaided-eye sketches without data links.
Satellites, robotic arms, observatories, and communication systems.
Weather stations, ocean sensors, and seismographs used mainly for Earth-surface monitoring.
Spectral labels, star names, and magnitude scales without physical instruments.
questions.explanation: Modern space science depends on many connected tools and systems.

questions.prompt: How can robotics connect to astronomy and space missions?
choices: Robotic systems are mainly useful for naming objects after observations are complete.
Robotics replaces scientific instruments by deciding conclusions before data are collected.
Robotics affects astronomy mostly by changing the colour classification of stars.
Robotic systems can help move, build, repair, or operate equipment in space.
questions.explanation: Robotic arms and automated systems are part of space mission technology.

questions.prompt: Why are communication systems important in space exploration?
choices: They convert radio signals directly into spacecraft fuel during long missions.
They allow spacecraft, satellites, and ground stations to exchange data and commands.
They remove the need for onboard instruments because ground stations make the measurements.
They are used mainly after a mission, when data no longer need to be transmitted.
questions.explanation: Without communication systems, mission data could not be reliably sent back or controlled.

questions.prompt: What two variables are commonly graphed in the H-R diagram investigation?
choices: Apparent magnitude and constellation name.
Spectral type and absolute magnitude.
Surface temperature and the number of planets in orbit.
Galaxy distance and redshift from the big bang unit.
questions.explanation: The investigation builds an H-R diagram from spectral type and absolute magnitude data.

questions.prompt: Why is plotting star data on a graph more useful than only reading a table?
choices: A graph changes the stars' actual luminosities so the main sequence becomes visible.
A graph can reveal patterns such as the main sequence more clearly.
A graph is mainly useful when the table has no measured values.
A graph makes spectral type unnecessary because the axes contain no labels.
questions.explanation: Visual patterns are often easier to identify on a graph.

questions.prompt: A student plots spectral type on one axis and absolute magnitude on the other. What pattern should they look for?
choices: A curve showing that apparent brightness increases with distance.
A cluster showing that spectral types have the same absolute magnitude.
A trend showing planets and stars on the same magnitude scale.
A main sequence trend through many of the plotted stars.
questions.explanation: The main sequence is a key pattern revealed by H-R diagrams.

questions.prompt: Which list gives the standard order of the main spectral type letters used in this investigation?
choices: \(A, B, F, G, K, M, O\), arranged alphabetically.
\(O, B, A, F, G, K, M\), arranged by spectral class.
\(G, K, M, O, B, A, F\), arranged from Sun-like to hottest.
\(O, B, A, C, D, E, F\), arranged as a simple letter sequence.
questions.explanation: Main sequence stars are often grouped using the spectral sequence O, B, A, F, G, K, M.

questions.prompt: What does a label such as \(G2\) or \(B5\) represent in the H-R investigation?
choices: A star's distance from Earth in light-years and parsecs.
A galaxy group label used to classify redshift measurements.
A spectral type with a letter category and number subdivision.
A ranking of apparent brightness without temperature information.
questions.explanation: The letter gives the spectral class, while the number provides a subdivision within that class.

questions.prompt: Why are spectral types useful when building an H-R diagram?
choices: They are used to place stars along a temperature- and colour-related axis.
They are used to calculate a star's distance without any brightness data.
They group stars by age rather than by light patterns.
They replace absolute magnitude because one axis is enough to make the diagram.
questions.explanation: Spectral type is linked to star colour and temperature patterns.

questions.prompt: How can a pattern on an H-R diagram help with prediction?
choices: It lets astronomers ignore whether the star belongs to the main sequence.
It predicts apparent brightness directly without considering distance.
It assigns the same absolute magnitude to stars with nearby spectral types.
It can be used to estimate absolute magnitude for a main sequence star from its spectral type.
questions.explanation: Once a trend is established, unknown or selected values can be estimated from the pattern.

questions.prompt: If a main sequence star's spectral type falls between two plotted values, what is a reasonable graph-based method?
choices: Use the apparent magnitude of the nearest bright star as the answer.
Estimate its absolute magnitude by interpolating along the main sequence trend.
Average the labels of two constellations near the star's position.
Treat the star as a red giant because graph predictions work better off the main sequence.
questions.explanation: Interpolation uses nearby plotted points to make a reasonable estimate.

questions.prompt: Which prediction task matches the H-R investigation most closely?
choices: Predict a galaxy's redshift from the name of a spectral class.
Estimate the absolute magnitude of a main sequence star such as a \(B5\) star.
Find a star's apparent brightness from a chart without knowing its distance.
Use the graph to decide whether a planet has moons.
questions.explanation: The investigation uses spectral type and a graph pattern to estimate absolute magnitude.

questions.prompt: Why are graphs important in astronomy?
choices: They remove measurement uncertainty when the pattern looks smooth.
They can reveal relationships that are difficult to see in a list of numbers.
They are useful mainly because they turn categories into exact causes.
They make unusual data points irrelevant to the scientific conclusion.
questions.explanation: Graphing data helps scientists notice trends, clusters, and exceptions.

questions.prompt: A set of star data forms a diagonal band on an H-R diagram. What claim could that support?
choices: The graph shows distance rather than luminosity because diagonal bands mean motion.
Many stars follow a main sequence pattern relating spectral type and brightness.
The stars in the band have identical masses and life-cycle stages.
The pattern is caused by plotting apparent brightness instead of absolute magnitude.
questions.explanation: The main sequence is a graph-based pattern in star properties.

questions.prompt: Which is the strongest graph-based reasoning habit?
choices: Use the plotted pattern as evidence and describe limits or uncertainty.
Choose the trend that best matches the expected answer before reading the axes.
Treat outliers as errors when they do not match the main sequence.
Assume a graph shows cause and effect when two variables line up.
questions.explanation: Good reasoning uses evidence while recognizing what the graph can and cannot show.

questions.prompt: What was the neutron star hypothesis before neutron stars were observed?
choices: A proposed idea that some supernovas could leave behind extremely dense neutron stars.
A claim that supernovas leave behind ordinary white dwarfs with no special density.
A model that treated neutron stars as bright main sequence stars with unusual spectra.
A naming system for compact objects before radio telescopes collected data.
questions.explanation: The idea of neutron stars was proposed before strong observational evidence was found.

questions.prompt: Why is the neutron star story a good example of scientific prediction?
choices: The model was accepted because it sounded unusual before evidence was available.
A theoretical idea later gained support from observations of pulsars and supernova remnants.
The prediction became scientific after researchers stopped looking for related observations.
The hypothesis was useful because it avoided connections to supernovas.
questions.explanation: Scientific ideas become stronger when independent evidence supports them.

questions.prompt: Which object is most closely connected to the neutron star hypothesis in this section?
choices: A supernova remnant with a compact dense object.
A red giant whose outer layers have not yet expanded.
A binary system used mainly to measure solar mass.
A main sequence star with a surface temperature similar to the Sun.
questions.explanation: Neutron stars are connected to the collapsed remnants of some supernovas.

questions.prompt: What role did radio telescope data play in Jocelyn Bell's discovery?
choices: Careful analysis of recorded radio signals revealed an unexpected repeating pattern.
The data were useful mainly because they matched the expected pattern immediately.
The radio records gave direct photographs of neutron stars in visible light.
The telescope data mattered because interpretation was unnecessary after recording.
questions.explanation: The discovery depended on noticing a small pattern in a large amount of data.

questions.prompt: Why can a small unusual signal in a large data set matter?
choices: It may reveal a real phenomenon that does not fit current expectations.
It is usually best treated as an error if it appears in a small part of the record.
It matters mainly because unusual signals are stronger than expected signals.
It can replace follow-up checks if its timing pattern looks regular.
questions.explanation: Unexpected patterns can lead to new questions and discoveries if checked carefully.

questions.prompt: Which scientific practice is highlighted by Bell's chart-paper analysis?
choices: Patient observation and careful pattern recognition.
Reducing a data set before checking whether small patterns repeat.
Changing the recorded signal after deciding what it should show.
Treating instrument records as complete explanations without discussion.
questions.explanation: The discovery required persistence and close attention to data.

questions.prompt: What is a pulsar?
choices: A source that emits regular pulses of radiation, later linked to neutron stars.
A red giant whose brightness changes because it moves along the main sequence.
A white dwarf that appears to pulse because its distance from Earth changes.
A radio telescope pattern produced when chart paper moves at a steady speed.
questions.explanation: Pulsars were identified through repeated radio signals and connected to neutron stars.

questions.prompt: Why did the regularity of early pulsar signals seem surprising?
choices: Such steady repeating signals were not expected from known celestial objects at the time.
The regular pattern matched the predicted behaviour of ordinary main sequence stars.
The signal was surprising because radio telescopes usually measure visible colours.
The pattern mattered because it showed the star had stopped rotating.
questions.explanation: The repeating pattern made scientists consider and test possible explanations.

questions.prompt: What evidence helped connect pulsars with neutron stars?
choices: Pulsars were found in contexts linked with supernova remnants, where neutron stars were expected.
Pulsars were identified as ordinary white dwarfs because their densities were low.
Pulsars were connected to neutron stars because they lacked repeating signals.
Pulsars were explained as redshifted galaxies moving away from Earth.
questions.explanation: The connection grew from multiple observations, including pulsars associated with supernova remnants.

questions.prompt: Why does Jocelyn Bell's role raise questions about recognition in science?
choices: Her careful work was central to the discovery, but major public recognition did not fully reflect that contribution.
Her role is debated because the discovery was made without recorded evidence.
The case matters because scientific discoveries are usually made without teams.
The issue is mainly about whether radio astronomy should count as astronomy.
questions.explanation: The case invites evaluation of how credit is assigned in scientific work.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes discovery in science?
choices: Discovery often depends on observation, interpretation, instruments, collaboration, and fair credit.
Discovery depends on the final publication more than on the observations that led to it.
Discovery is usually complete as soon as an unexpected signal appears in data.
Discovery becomes scientific when social questions about credit are ignored.
questions.explanation: Scientific achievements usually involve both evidence and human systems of collaboration and recognition.

questions.prompt: What is a fair question to ask when evaluating a scientific discovery story?
choices: Who collected, interpreted, supported, and communicated the evidence?
Which person held the highest position before the evidence was found?
Which part of the evidence can be left out to make the story simpler?
How can collaboration be described as a single person's work?
questions.explanation: Fair evaluation looks at the real contributions that made the discovery possible.

questions.prompt: What does the big bang theory try to explain?
choices: The origin of the universe and how it has changed over time.
The life cycle of one star from nebula to white dwarf.
The order of planets within the Solar System.
The classification of galaxies by visible shape.
questions.explanation: The theory connects evidence about expansion with the universe's history and development.

questions.prompt: Which observation is most directly connected to the big bang theory in this chapter?
choices: Evidence that the universe is expanding.
Evidence that planets orbit stars in a flat disk.
Evidence that main sequence stars follow a diagonal H-R pattern.
Evidence that constellations appear in seasonal patterns.
questions.explanation: Expansion evidence is central to the modern big bang model.

questions.prompt: Which statement best avoids a misconception about the big bang theory?
choices: It is a scientific explanation supported and refined by evidence, not just a casual guess.
It describes an explosion from a central point into space that was already there.
It explains the Solar System separately from the larger universe.
It is based mainly on the brightness of nearby stars rather than large-scale evidence.
questions.explanation: The big bang theory is an evidence-based scientific model of the universe's history.

questions.prompt: What does cosmology study?
choices: The origin, structure, development, and possible future of the universe.
The formation of planets within one solar system.
The classification of stars by spectral type on an H-R diagram.
The motion of spacecraft within Earth's orbit.
questions.explanation: Cosmology is the study of the universe as a whole.

questions.prompt: How is the big bang theory connected to cosmology?
choices: It is a diagram used to classify individual main sequence stars.
It is a scientific model used to explain the universe's origin and development.
It is a method for measuring the mass of planets in our Solar System.
It is a weather model for conditions in Earth's upper atmosphere.
questions.explanation: The big bang theory is part of modern scientific cosmology.

questions.prompt: Which question is most cosmological?
choices: How does a single red giant compare with the Sun's future stage?
How can a satellite keep communicating with one ground station?
How does a spectral type label place one star on an H-R diagram?
How has the universe changed since its earliest stages?
questions.explanation: Cosmology asks broad questions about the universe's history and structure.

questions.prompt: What happens in the Doppler effect?
choices: A source changes its actual colour because its temperature changes.
A wave becomes brighter because the observer uses a larger telescope.
Waves from a moving source are compressed or stretched as seen by an observer.
A galaxy's distance is measured directly from its apparent size.
questions.explanation: Relative motion changes the observed wavelength of waves.

questions.prompt: How can the Doppler effect help astronomers?
choices: It lets them infer whether a star or galaxy is moving toward or away from us.
It gives absolute magnitude without needing distance or luminosity data.
It explains star colour by composition rather than by motion.
It measures the age of the universe from one nearby star.
questions.explanation: Shifts in spectral lines can reveal motion along the line of sight.

questions.prompt: A light source moving away has its light waves stretched. What kind of wavelength change is this?
choices: A shift toward shorter wavelengths.
A shift toward longer wavelengths.
A change in brightness with no wavelength shift.
A change in spectral lines caused by new elements forming.
questions.explanation: Moving away stretches observed wavelengths.

questions.prompt: What does redshift usually indicate for a distant galaxy?
choices: The galaxy is moving toward the observer along the line of sight.
The galaxy is moving away from the observer as space expands.
The galaxy contains mostly cool red stars, regardless of motion.
The galaxy's light has become brighter because it is nearby.
questions.explanation: Redshift means spectral lines are shifted toward longer, redder wavelengths.

questions.prompt: If spectral lines shift toward longer wavelengths, what term describes the shift?
choices: Blueshift.
Redshift.
Parallax.
Solar mass.
questions.explanation: Longer visible wavelengths are associated with the red end of the spectrum.

questions.prompt: Why was widespread galaxy redshift important evidence?
choices: It suggested many distant galaxies are moving away, supporting an expanding universe.
It showed that red stars are older than blue stars in nearby galaxies.
It proved that galaxies are fixed in space while Earth moves away from them.
It showed that the Milky Way contains the rest of the universe.
questions.explanation: Redshift patterns helped scientists infer cosmic expansion.

questions.prompt: What does blueshift indicate about an object in space?
choices: It is moving away from the observer while its light is stretched.
It is moving toward the observer.
It has become physically hotter and therefore looks blue.
It is nearby enough that spectral line shifts are less important than colour.
questions.explanation: Blueshift occurs when observed wavelengths are shortened toward the blue end of the spectrum.

questions.prompt: A star's spectral lines shift toward shorter wavelengths. What is the best interpretation?
choices: The star is moving toward the observer along the line of sight.
The star is cooling into a red giant even though its lines shift blueward.
The star is moving sideways with no motion toward or away from the observer.
The star's spectrum is changing because its elements have disappeared.
questions.explanation: Shorter observed wavelengths correspond to blueshift.

questions.prompt: Which comparison between redshift and blueshift is correct?
choices: Redshift and blueshift both describe changes in apparent brightness, not wavelength.
Redshift is longer wavelength from moving away; blueshift is shorter wavelength from moving toward.
Redshift means motion toward Earth; blueshift means motion away from Earth.
Both shifts are caused by star colour changing without motion.
questions.explanation: The direction of wavelength change tells astronomers about motion toward or away.

questions.prompt: What does Hubble's law show?
choices: A galaxy's speed away from us is proportional to its distance.
A galaxy's apparent size directly gives its mass.
Nearby planets are responsible for the redshift of distant galaxies.
Galaxy distance is unrelated to recession speed.
questions.explanation: Hubble's law links recession speed and distance.

questions.prompt: A galaxy twice as far away tends to move away about twice as fast in a simple Hubble-law pattern. What does this support?
choices: The idea that Earth pushes distant galaxies outward by gravity.
The idea that space itself is expanding.
The idea that redshift is caused mainly by telescope error.
The idea that the Milky Way sits at the physical centre of an explosion.
questions.explanation: A proportional speed-distance pattern is evidence for expansion of space.

questions.prompt: On a graph of galaxy velocity versus distance, what does the slope represent in this context?
choices: The Hubble constant, or expansion rate.
The average apparent magnitude of the galaxies.
The age of each galaxy plotted on the graph.
The difference between redshift and blueshift in one star.
questions.explanation: The slope of the velocity-distance graph gives the Hubble constant.

questions.prompt: What is cosmic microwave background radiation?
choices: Leftover radiation from the early universe that now appears mostly as microwaves.
Radio noise made by modern satellites orbiting Earth.
Visible light from nearby stars blended across the night sky.
Heat released by galaxies after their stars finish evolving.
questions.explanation: CMB radiation is a major piece of evidence for the hot early universe.

questions.prompt: Why did early radiation become microwave radiation over time?
choices: Galaxies absorbed the radiation and re-emitted it as ordinary starlight.
Earth's atmosphere cooled the radiation after telescopes detected it.
The radiation changed because stars began producing heavier elements.
Expansion stretched its wavelengths into cooler, longer-wavelength parts of the spectrum.
questions.explanation: As the universe expanded, wavelengths of radiation were stretched.

questions.prompt: What did COBE and WMAP help measure?
choices: Tiny temperature variations in the cosmic microwave background.
The masses of stars by timing binary star orbits.
The redshift of one nearby galaxy to calculate solar mass.
The brightness changes of Sun-like stars over several weeks.
questions.explanation: Satellite maps of the CMB gave detailed evidence about the early universe.

questions.prompt: Why does looking farther into space mean looking farther back in time?
choices: Light has a finite speed, so distant light left its source long ago.
Telescopes reverse time directly when pointed at faint galaxies.
Distant galaxies are younger because distance slows their clocks for observers.
Space has distance, but light from far objects reaches Earth immediately.
questions.explanation: The farther light travels, the older the information it carries.

questions.prompt: If a galaxy is 6 billion light-years away, what are we seeing?
choices: The galaxy exactly as it is at this instant in our frame of reference.
The galaxy as it appears after its light travelled for about 6 billion years.
A galaxy that is nearby because light-years measure brightness.
A galaxy from before the universe began expanding.
questions.explanation: A light-year is tied to how long light has travelled.

questions.prompt: Why are distant galaxies useful for studying the early universe?
choices: Their light shows earlier stages because it has taken a long time to reach us.
Their redshift makes them physically closer to the big bang than nearby galaxies are.
Their apparent size directly gives the age of the universe.
Their light arrives before it is emitted because space is expanding.
questions.explanation: Distant observations give evidence from earlier cosmic times.

questions.prompt: What early stage does the CMB help scientists observe?
choices: A time when the young universe had cooled enough for photons to travel freely.
The first instant of the big bang, before any expansion occurred.
The moment when the Sun and planets began forming.
The stage when modern galaxies had already reached their present forms.
questions.explanation: The CMB is light from an early stage when the universe became transparent to photons.

questions.prompt: About how old was the universe when the CMB light could travel freely?
choices: About 8 minutes old.
About 380 000 years old.
About 4.5 billion years old.
About 100 trillion years old.
questions.explanation: The checklist identifies the CMB with the universe at about 380 000 years old.

questions.prompt: Why is the CMB seen in all directions?
choices: It comes from one nearby source that moves across the sky each day.
It is reflected starlight from planets in the Solar System.
It is produced by telescope receivers rather than by the universe.
It is radiation from the early universe that fills space around us.
questions.explanation: CMB radiation is a background signal from the early universe.

questions.prompt: What does inflation describe in early-universe models?
choices: An extremely rapid expansion very soon after the big bang.
A gradual cooling of Earth after the Moon formed.
A stage when stars expanded into red giants.
A steady-state model in which the universe does not change.
questions.explanation: Inflation refers to a very brief and rapid early expansion.

questions.prompt: Why is early expansion an important step in the universe's timeline?
choices: It explains how individual planets settled into stable orbits around the Sun.
It shows that galaxies were moving through a fixed empty space before time began.
It helps explain how the universe grew from extremely small, hot conditions toward larger-scale structure.
It describes why main sequence stars burn hydrogen at different rates.
questions.explanation: Expansion is central to connecting the early universe with later cosmic structure.

questions.prompt: Which statement best avoids a misconception about the big bang?
choices: It describes expansion of the universe itself, not an ordinary explosion into pre-existing empty space.
It happened from the centre of the Milky Way and pushed galaxies outward.
It explains the origin of the Solar System but not the larger universe.
It was a brief event after which cosmic expansion stopped.
questions.explanation: Cosmic expansion is not the same as fragments flying through ordinary space from one point.

questions.prompt: What happened as the early universe expanded?
choices: It cooled, allowing particles and later atoms to form.
It stayed at the same temperature while galaxies formed immediately.
It formed the present-day Solar System before particles existed.
It cooled by losing energy outside the universe into surrounding space.
questions.explanation: Expansion and cooling allowed matter to form in stages.

questions.prompt: Why could photons travel freely only after the universe cooled enough?
choices: Photons were created by modern telescopes when scientists detected the CMB.
Gravity stopped acting during this stage, letting light move without resistance.
The universe became transparent because stars had already formed everywhere.
Electrons could bind with atoms, so light was no longer constantly scattered by charged particles.
questions.explanation: When atoms formed, radiation could travel through space more freely.

questions.prompt: Which sequence best matches the general idea of early-universe development?
choices: Hot dense conditions -> expansion and cooling -> particles and atoms -> larger structures later.
Modern galaxies -> hot dense beginning -> atoms -> expansion.
Solar System formation -> first atoms -> CMB -> earliest galaxies.
Stars and planets -> elementary particles -> expansion begins.
questions.explanation: The universe changed from very hot early conditions toward matter and later cosmic structures.

kp-553.title: KP5: Formation of stars and galaxies

questions.prompt: When did the earliest stars and first galaxies form in the big bang timeline?
choices: After the early universe cooled enough for matter to clump under gravity.
During the earliest hot stage, before particles and atoms could form.
After the Solar System formed and supplied the first heavy elements.
At the same time as the CMB, before matter could collect into larger structures.
questions.explanation: Stars and galaxies formed after earlier cooling and matter formation stages.

questions.prompt: Why does gravity matter in the formation of stars and galaxies?
choices: It spreads matter evenly so clumps have difficulty becoming stars.
It pulls matter together into larger structures.
It changes microwave radiation into the first atoms.
It mainly affects planets after stars and galaxies are already complete.
questions.explanation: Gravity helps gas and matter collect into stars, galaxies, and larger structures.

questions.prompt: Which sequence best places star and galaxy formation in cosmic history?
choices: CMB -> Solar System -> elementary particles -> first galaxies.
Earliest stars -> big bang -> cooling -> first atoms.
Big bang -> cooling and matter formation -> earliest stars and galaxies.
Modern galaxies -> first atoms -> expansion begins.
questions.explanation: Stars and galaxies are later than the earliest hot, dense stages of the universe.

questions.prompt: How does solar system formation fit into the universe's timeline?
choices: The Solar System formed during the same early stage recorded by the CMB.
The Sun and Solar System formed much later than the earliest stars and galaxies.
The Solar System formed before earlier stars enriched space with heavier elements.
The Solar System was the first structure produced after expansion began.
questions.explanation: The Solar System is a relatively late part of cosmic history.

questions.prompt: Why is Earth considered a late part of cosmic history?
choices: Many stars and galaxies existed long before the Sun and planets formed.
Earth formed when photons first began travelling freely through space.
Earth formed before the first stars because planets are simpler structures.
Earth formed from CMB radiation without needing earlier stars or nebulae.
questions.explanation: The universe had already gone through many stages before the Solar System formed.

questions.prompt: Which misconception should be avoided?
choices: The Solar System formed before the earliest stars because planets are smaller.
Stars and galaxies existed before the Solar System.
The Sun and planets formed after many earlier cosmic events.
The big bang was not simply the formation of Earth.
questions.explanation: The big bang theory describes the universe's history, not just Earth's beginning.

questions.prompt: How do powerful telescopes help scientists study the early universe?
choices: They observe extremely distant galaxies whose light left long ago.
They recreate early-universe temperatures inside the telescope mirror.
They make nearby galaxies appear older by increasing magnification.
They replace redshift and CMB evidence with direct travel to the past.
questions.explanation: Distant light lets astronomers study earlier stages of cosmic history.

questions.prompt: Why are particle accelerators useful for early-universe questions?
choices: They observe distant galaxies by collecting visible light from space.
They slow cosmic expansion so early-universe conditions can be measured directly.
They can create high-energy conditions that help scientists test ideas about very early matter.
They classify galaxies by shape using particle tracks.
questions.explanation: High-energy experiments can model some conditions related to the early universe.

questions.prompt: Which pairing best fits this knowledge point?
choices: James Webb Space Telescope: early galaxies; CERN Large Hadron Collider: high-energy particle conditions.
James Webb Space Telescope: particle collisions; CERN: distant galaxy imaging.
James Webb Space Telescope: local weather; CERN: measuring galaxy redshift.
James Webb Space Telescope and CERN: unrelated to evidence about the early universe.
questions.explanation: Different technologies investigate different kinds of evidence about the early universe.

questions.prompt: Why do scientists still describe the universe as containing major mysteries?
choices: The big bang theory explains observations so completely that further evidence is unnecessary.
Modern telescopes detect visible matter, so unseen effects no longer need explanations.
Cosmology has stopped changing because the main questions were settled in the 1930s.
New evidence has answered many questions, but dark matter, dark energy, size, age, and evolution still raise problems.
questions.explanation: Modern cosmology has strong evidence but also important unanswered questions.

questions.prompt: What happens to scientific explanations when new evidence appears?
choices: They can be refined, expanded, or revised.
They are kept unchanged if they are already written in a textbook.
They become less scientific if technology improves the data.
They are replaced by guesses before the new evidence is tested.
questions.explanation: Science changes as evidence and technology improve.

questions.prompt: Which attitude best fits this section?
choices: We know a lot, but continued observation and interpretation are still needed.
Unanswered questions show that existing evidence should be ignored.
Better technology matters mainly for collecting prettier images.
Visible matter is enough to explain the motion and expansion evidence.
questions.explanation: The section emphasizes both progress and open questions.

questions.prompt: What is dark matter?
choices: Matter inferred from gravitational effects even though it does not emit light we can detect directly.
Ordinary gas and dust that block visible light from background stars.
A form of starlight too dim for unaided-eye observation.
The unknown cause of the accelerating expansion of the universe.
questions.explanation: Dark matter is inferred from gravity, not directly seen by emitted light.

questions.prompt: Why do astronomers infer dark matter in galaxies?
choices: Visible matter has no gravitational effect on stars in galaxies.
Observed motions suggest more mass than visible matter alone can explain.
The colour of a galaxy directly shows how much dark matter it contains.
Dark matter is assumed when a galaxy is too far away to photograph clearly.
questions.explanation: Galaxy rotation and cluster motion point to additional unseen mass.

questions.prompt: Which statement avoids a common misconception?
choices: Dark matter is not called dark because it is black-coloured visible matter; it is inferred because it does not emit detectable light.
Dark matter is the same as a shadow cast by bright stars and galaxies.
Dark matter affects galaxy motion mainly by absorbing starlight rather than through gravity.
Dark matter has been identified in enough detail that its particles are fully known.
questions.explanation: The name refers to missing unseen mass, not simply colour.

questions.prompt: What unexpected observation in Andromeda supports the idea of missing mass?
choices: Its visible light shows no evidence that stars orbit the galaxy's centre.
Its stars move more slowly than gravity predicts from visible matter.
Its stars move faster than predicted from visible matter alone.
Its spectrum shows redshift but no information about star motion.
questions.explanation: Faster-than-expected star motion suggests extra unseen mass.

questions.prompt: Why was visible light from Andromeda not enough to explain its motion?
choices: The visible matter estimated from light did not provide enough mass to match observed star speeds.
Visible light measures temperature, so it is separate from gravitational predictions.
The galaxy's visible stars were moving too slowly to require any unseen matter.
Andromeda's apparent brightness showed that mass was distributed evenly at each radius.
questions.explanation: The gravitational effects were larger than visible matter could account for.

questions.prompt: Which reasoning pattern matches this evidence?
choices: Prediction from visible mass -> observed speeds too high -> infer additional unseen mass.
Observed speeds too high -> reject gravity -> stop comparing with mass.
Visible light faint -> assume the galaxy has no matter outside its centre.
Redshift detected -> conclude the galaxy is made mostly of dark energy.
questions.explanation: Dark matter is inferred by comparing predictions with observations.

questions.prompt: What is a dark matter halo?
choices: A bright ring of ordinary stars that marks a galaxy's visible edge.
A large region of inferred dark matter surrounding a galaxy.
A cloud of dust that blocks a galaxy's centre from view.
A background glow from the CMB around nearby galaxies.
questions.explanation: Dark matter is thought to extend in large halos around galaxies.

questions.prompt: Why do galaxy groups and clusters suggest dark matter halos?
choices: Their motions often require more gravitational mass than visible matter shows.
Their visible stars are so bright that gravity becomes unimportant.
Their galaxy motions can be explained by apparent brightness without mass.
Their redshift shows that dark energy and dark matter are the same thing.
questions.explanation: Large-scale motions point to unseen gravitational mass around galaxies and clusters.

questions.prompt: Which observation would most support a halo of unseen matter?
choices: Objects orbit as if a galaxy has much more mass than its visible stars and gas.
A galaxy appears redder because many of its stars are cool.
A star's spectral type places it on the main sequence.
A galaxy is difficult to see because of city light pollution.
questions.explanation: Orbital motion can reveal gravitational mass that is not directly visible.

questions.prompt: What is dark energy?
choices: A name for the unknown cause of the accelerating expansion of the universe.
The invisible matter inferred from galaxy rotation speeds.
Energy released by stars during ordinary nuclear fusion.
Radiation left over from the early universe.
questions.explanation: Dark energy is used as a name for whatever is driving accelerated cosmic expansion.

questions.prompt: Why were Type Ia supernovae useful in inferring dark energy?
choices: They directly measured dark matter particles inside galaxy halos.
They showed that redshift is caused by ordinary star colour rather than expansion.
Their explosions happened near Earth, making cosmic expansion easier to ignore.
Their known brightness helped reveal they were farther away than expected from a slowing-expansion model.
questions.explanation: Type Ia supernova observations provided evidence that expansion is accelerating.

questions.prompt: Which statement is most accurate?
choices: Dark energy is inferred from observations, but its true nature is still unknown.
Dark energy is the same material as dark matter, just farther away.
Dark energy is ordinary starlight that has been shifted into the microwave range.
Dark energy is a completed explanation with no remaining uncertainty.
questions.explanation: The evidence suggests an effect, but the cause remains mysterious.

questions.prompt: What did astronomers originally expect gravity to do to cosmic expansion?
choices: Slow it down over time.
Speed it up by pushing galaxies away from one another.
Make redshift disappear from distant galaxy spectra.
Keep distant galaxies at fixed distances from Earth.
questions.explanation: Gravity attracts matter, so scientists expected expansion to slow.

questions.prompt: What surprising pattern did evidence from distant Type Ia supernovae suggest?
choices: The universe's expansion began accelerating billions of years ago.
The universe's expansion was slowing exactly as gravity predicted.
The redshift-distance pattern applies mainly inside the Solar System.
Type Ia supernovae are too dim to compare with distance models.
questions.explanation: Supernova data suggested acceleration rather than continued slowing.

questions.prompt: Why did accelerating expansion require a new explanatory idea?
choices: It did not fit the expectation that gravity alone should slow expansion.
It showed that gravity works mainly near planets and less near galaxies.
It removed the need for redshift evidence in cosmology.
It meant dark matter and visible matter were identical.
questions.explanation: The acceleration suggested that another effect, called dark energy, was involved.

kp-562.title: KP7: Visible matter as a small fraction

questions.prompt: According to current estimates in the checklist, what makes up most of the universe?
choices: Dark energy and dark matter.
Visible stars, planets, and glowing gas.
The matter inside the Milky Way galaxy.
CMB radiation and ordinary starlight.
questions.explanation: Visible matter is only a small fraction compared with dark matter and dark energy.

questions.prompt: Why is the visible universe not the same as everything that exists?
choices: Much of the universe is inferred from effects such as gravity and expansion rather than directly seen light.
Visible matter is an illusion caused by telescope limits.
Dark matter is brighter than stars but hidden by Earth's atmosphere.
Unseen components are included mainly because they make the model simpler.
questions.explanation: Observations show effects that visible matter alone cannot explain.

questions.prompt: If visible matter is only about \(4\%\) of the universe in one estimate, which statement follows?
choices: Visible stars and galaxies provide enough mass-energy to explain the universe.
Most of the universe's mass-energy is not ordinary visible stars and galaxies.
Dark matter and dark energy are minor labels for ordinary planets.
The estimate means the rest of the universe is empty space with no effects.
questions.explanation: The estimate emphasizes how much remains unseen or not fully understood.

questions.prompt: Which topic is Stephen Hawking especially connected with in this checklist section?
choices: Ideas about black holes, matter, time, and space.
The construction of the first optical telescope used by Galileo.
The measurement of galaxy rotation curves that led to dark matter.
The classification of main sequence stars by spectral type.
questions.explanation: The extension connects Hawking with major ideas about black holes and the nature of the universe.

questions.prompt: Why is Hawking's work relevant to Unit 4?
choices: It focused on replacing evidence-based astronomy with personal opinion.
It contributed to changing scientific ideas about extreme objects and the universe.
It mainly explained planet formation inside our Solar System.
It showed that black holes are ordinary main sequence stars.
questions.explanation: His work is part of the broader development of ideas about space, time, and black holes.

questions.prompt: Which statement best connects individual scientists to scientific knowledge?
choices: Individual insights can influence how scientific communities interpret evidence and build models.
A scientist's reputation is stronger evidence than observations or models.
A single idea becomes final once the first scientist communicates it.
Individual work matters mainly when it avoids discussion with the wider community.
questions.explanation: Science develops through evidence, models, communication, and contributions from individuals and communities.

questions.prompt: What scientific attitude does Hawking's story highlight?
choices: Choosing simple questions so scientific ideas rarely need revision.
Persistence and curiosity despite major challenges.
Treating communication as separate from scientific understanding.
Avoiding theoretical models when direct observation is difficult.
questions.explanation: The extension presents persistence and curiosity as important qualities in science.

questions.prompt: Why can perseverance matter in science?
choices: Difficult questions may require long-term thinking, revision, and effort.
It allows scientists to keep an explanation unchanged after new evidence appears.
It matters mainly when evidence is too weak to discuss openly.
It replaces the need for collaboration, instruments, and review.
questions.explanation: Scientific work often involves sustained effort through uncertainty and obstacles.

questions.prompt: Which response best reflects scientific perseverance?
choices: Continue refining ideas and methods when a problem is hard.
Keep using the first model even when evidence points elsewhere.
Focus on confidence in the answer rather than on testing it.
Avoid sharing uncertain results because revision is part of science.
questions.explanation: Perseverance supports careful inquiry rather than giving up or ignoring evidence.

questions.prompt: Why is communicating complex science important?
choices: It turns an uncertain model into a proven fact for the public.
It matters mainly after scientific evidence has stopped changing.
It simplifies science by removing the need to discuss limits.
It helps more people understand and think about scientific ideas.
questions.explanation: Public communication can make difficult ideas more accessible and inspire curiosity.

questions.prompt: Which example best shows communication of complex ideas?
choices: Explaining black holes and the universe in language non-specialists can engage with.
Presenting equations without context so the audience has difficulty questioning them.
Reducing a scientific idea until its evidence and limits disappear.
Separating public interest from the scientific model being explained.
questions.explanation: Good communication connects complex concepts to clear explanations.

questions.prompt: Which misconception should be avoided?
choices: Communicating science is part of science culture, not a separate activity with no value.
Science communication is valuable mainly when it avoids difficult concepts.
Public interest in astronomy is separate from how scientific ideas spread.
Clear explanations reduce accuracy when they use everyday language.
questions.explanation: Science communication can preserve accuracy while widening understanding.

questions.prompt: What relationship is used in the age-of-the-universe investigation?
choices: A star's spectral class directly gives the age of the universe.
A galaxy's redshift is related to its distance from Earth.
A galaxy's apparent brightness replaces the need for redshift data.
A planet's orbital period gives the Hubble constant.
questions.explanation: The investigation uses redshift and distance data to explore cosmic expansion.

questions.prompt: How can redshifted spectral lines be used in this investigation?
choices: They help estimate a galaxy's velocity away from Earth.
They show the galaxy's distance directly without using a graph.
They identify the galaxy's shape but not its motion.
They replace the need to compare the shifted lines with reference positions.
questions.explanation: The amount of redshift can be connected to recession velocity.

questions.prompt: A galaxy has a larger redshift than another galaxy in the same data set. What does that usually suggest?
choices: It is moving away faster.
It is closer but has cooler stars.
It is moving toward us with a stronger blueshift.
It has the same velocity but a different chemical composition.
questions.explanation: Greater redshift generally corresponds to greater recession velocity.

questions.prompt: On a graph of galaxy velocity versus galaxy distance, what gives the Hubble constant \(H\)?
choices: The average of the galaxy names used in the table.
The y-intercept without considering the data trend.
The slope of the line of best fit.
The largest distance value divided by the smallest velocity value.
questions.explanation: The Hubble constant is found from the slope of velocity versus distance.

questions.prompt: A line rises from \(3000\,km/s\) to \(9000\,km/s\) while distance increases from \(50\,Mpc\) to \(150\,Mpc\). What is the slope?
Why is a line of best fit useful when estimating \(H\)?
choices: It summarizes the overall trend in data that may not fall perfectly on one line.
It turns scattered data into exact values without uncertainty.
It changes the measured galaxy velocities to match the expected answer.
It makes units such as \(km/s/Mpc\) unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Real data vary, so a best-fit line helps estimate the overall relationship.

kp-568.title: KP3: Estimating the age of the universe

questions.prompt: Which formula is used in the checklist to estimate the age of the universe from \(H\)?
questions.explanation: The investigation uses \(10^{12}/H\) as an estimate of cosmic age in years.

questions.prompt: If \(H=50\), what age estimate does \(\frac{10^{12}}{H}\) give?
questions.explanation: \(10^{12}/50 = 2 \times 10^{10}\), or 20 billion years.

questions.prompt: Why is this age calculation called an estimate?
choices: It depends on measured data, a best-fit line, and a simplified relationship.
It is an estimate because it avoids using measured galaxy data.
It gives a value for Earth's age rather than the universe's age.
It becomes exact once one galaxy's velocity is known.
questions.explanation: The method is useful but depends on assumptions and measurement uncertainty.

questions.prompt: Why do astronomers use megaparsecs in Hubble-law graphs?
choices: They are convenient units for extremely large galaxy distances.
They measure recession velocity instead of distance.
They are used because light-years are too large to compare with galaxies.
They describe the temperature change caused by redshift.
questions.explanation: Megaparsecs are useful for very large distances between galaxies.

questions.prompt: What does the unit \(km/s/Mpc\) describe for the Hubble constant?
choices: Velocity increase per megaparsec of distance.
Distance travelled by light in one megaparsec per second.
Mass of dark matter per galaxy cluster.
Brightness change per spectral class.
questions.explanation: The Hubble constant compares recession velocity with distance.

questions.prompt: Why is velocity used instead of speed alone in the redshift investigation?
choices: Galaxy motion has direction: toward Earth or away from Earth.
Velocity describes apparent brightness rather than motion.
Speed is used for planets, while velocity is used for stars by convention.
